focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 13

Four chalk headings waited on the blackboard when Halima stepped into the schoolroom: NAME, RELATION, YEARS IN HOUSE, WITNESSES. Aminul had pushed the children's benches into two hard ranks beneath them. The room smelled of old dust, damp jute, and the sour chalk children sucked when the teacher's back was turned. There had been no rain since yesterday, but the river still made itself known. From the eastern side of the char came, now and then, the low collapsing sound of bank giving way somewhere out of sight, as if a huge wet mat were being dragged over earth.

Nura sat at the teacher's table with ruled paper stacked by her elbow, the small burn mark on her wrist showing each time she dipped her pen. She had tied her hair back too tight. Since the trip to the neighboring char she had moved with a colder economy, as if she had spent a day looking for a father and come back with no use left for softness. She did not look up when Halima came in. She only said, "He's made groups," and slid her chin toward Aminul.

Aminul stood near the board with his folders held a little off his shirtfront to save them from sweat. Even in this heat he had managed a pressed collar. Chalk dust sat on one cuff. "The clerk sent word from the mainland," he said. "The magistrate may come the day after tomorrow. If not then, the next morning. We don't have room for confusion now."

Halima put her palm on the nearest bench. The wood still held the sticky warmth of children. "A house isn't an exam answer," she said.

"No," Aminul said. "But if five people from one yard give me five versions, the affidavit will tear in the first question."

He had arranged the first pile already, with weak papers on top and difficult dependencies underneath, the households most likely to split open w...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...came a boy Halima knew by face but not by name, sent by his mother from the west side, asking if a blind grandmother counted. After him came Hasina again, this time without speaking, only tapping the house-affidavit heading once with her forefinger and leaving before anyone could see. After her came no one for several minutes.

Across the schoolyard, near the path to the eastern ground, Mofiz had formed his own small queue without ever calling it one. Men stood with him holding document sleeves and tax receipts, their bodies arranged in a curve of caution around his white clothes. Jamila and Majed were there now. Majed held the papers. Jamila held herself.

Blood list, Halima thought. House list. The phrases would harden by dark.

Aminul said quietly, "If the magistrate asks later who encouraged which route, I will have to answer truthfully."

"Then answer it," Halima said.

"You understand what that means."

She looked past him. Mofiz had turned his little circle inward, yet every few breaths his eyes slid back to Nura's page. Jamila stood in that curve with Majed. Rahima stood at Halima's shoulder. Between the two groups, a strip of trampled schoolyard lay empty.

She put one broad palm flat on the exercise book so the breeze would not lift the page. The cheap paper was warm from the sun and rough under her skin.

"Let them say it," she said.

Down by the ghat, somebody shouted for more tea. A child took up the new words again, turning them into play before the adults could stop him.

"House list."

"Blood list."

Across the yard, one of Mofiz's men laughed. On Halima's side of the veranda, Rahima stood with her bad heel half out of the sandal and did not move. Another woman was already coming up the lane with a folded paper in one hand and a little girl in the other.
